channel.title: Game Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Game Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: AD&D Design Pillars
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Advanced Dungeons and Dragons design pillars.

I’ve talked about design pillars on this channel. I have a whole video on it. I’ll link it below.

In short, these pillars are like the bedrock, the foundation of your game, from which all features and all content spring from and to which they serve. It’s basically you saying: “This is essentially what my game is about, and everything I’m going to talk about — from a mechanic to the storyline, to elements of the setting — will make sense with respect to those pillars. I want to talk about what AD and D’s design pillars were, why it was my first tabletop role playing game. It was the very first thing that told me what a role playing game could be, which, of course, very much influenced my career, but its design pillars were never clearly stated anywhere. The game itself sprang from strategy tabletop games, where they were essentially battle simulations and occasionally a hero character would like emerge from that. You’d have a bunch of battles and there was one surviving lieutenant or captain that survived battle after battle after battle and you kind, you were kind of starting to get attached to them and a uh, Dungeons and Dragons, and then later AD and D, which formalized a lot of uh, D and D’s rules, were people wanting to play characters from those sessions and have them become heroes where they would, you know, go into dungeons and fight villains and gain all kinds of loot and maybe get their castle and become a hero From all this. What I always took from Dn D as its fundamental pillar was, with reference to fantasy, because that’s what it was aimed at — is that a any character you could think of, whether you liked Lord of the Rings or whether you like the Hiboreian age, if you wanted to play Aragorn or you wanted.

item.title: Evolution Of Design Documentation
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the evolution of design documentation.

This actually comes from a question from Noah Stavish, who asked: “Hey Tim, can you discuss what you expect out of design and technical documentation, both when you write it and when you review what others have written? Can you also touch on how teamwide expectations for documentation has changed over the past 40 years? What problems and solutions have you faced with these expectations?

item.title: One More Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making one more game.

And I will get to what I’m talking about in a second, because it’s a fun Friday. So this is going to be a good fun Friday video. But I want to start with this. I’m on the record of saying I don’t know what gamers want or like in a game. I have a whole video called Do Devs Know What Gamers Like. Now in that video, a lot of people chose to misinterpret it, despite the fact that I was very clear. So let me restate this: I think that you know what you want, but I don’t think that most people — people, gamers or game devs — know what most people want.

item.title: Lenses
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about lenses.

I’ve mentioned this before, and I’m going to make this a fun Friday video because it’s going to be kind of circular and in the middle of it I’m going to talk a lot about insomnia and then I’ll be back. So I’ve talked about lenses before. Lenses are the perspective that you and I and everyone tend to see the world through. Everybody has them, You have them. I talk mostly on this you know channel, about role playing games and player agency within them and nonlinearity of, because that’s my lens.

item.title: Design Potholes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about design potholes.

This is something I mentioned in a video about I need it will be 5 weeks ago when you’re watching this. I mentioned it in my video on un-retirement, which I’ll link below. And I had a couple commenters on that video, namely Saber VS 7 and Leah from. There’s a lot of M’s in that name Basically wanted to know what I meant by design potholes and they said: can you talk about it? And, like me, talk about something crazy. So of course I’m going to talk about design potholes in detail. What I mean by the term is I call pothole a design pothole — really common design issues that can be avoided if you know to look for them. They’re like a pothole on a regular street. If you go slowly, you’re not really going to be hurt that much by a pothole, or your car is not going to be hurt. But if you’re cruising along and you hit a pothole, at a pretty decent speed, you could wreck your transmission or your, or you blow out a tire or something. But once you’ve been up and down that road a few times and you know those potholes are there, you know which lane is safe, or even in a lane where you can be positioned so that pothole is not a problem for you. That’s what these design potholes are. They’re really common design issues that are frequently occur in different lanes of design, whether they’re system design or narrative design or level design.

item.title: Overanalyzing Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about overanalyzing games.

This is something a lot of people do. They like to take a game and analyze it. But if I want to be less charitable, they like to take a game and they like to pick at it and just pick at it and pick at it. Sometimes they do that without even playing the game. They heard about it, They heard a description, they saw a trailer.

item.title: Randomness In Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about randomness in games.

This is a question from Salute, the Captain, who said: “I was surprised to not find a video on randomness as a general mechanic on your channel. What are your thoughts on balancing out? Everything is too static and this feels unfair — weird random complaints”. So I did talk about how I’ve gone about in the past implementing features that used randomness and I’ll link that video below. The one thing that came out of it that I I could not stress highly enough in that video was that other game developers are terrible at what they mean by randomness. They will say randomness. That word will come out of their mouth repeatedly, even though I push back and I’m like “That’s not exactly what you want”. And I tried to explain to them what they want. They know I want it — random. Turns out they don’t want random. They want fairness and uniformity and cyclic behavior.

item.title: Toys And Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about toys and games.

This is a fun Friday. In fact, for me it’s New Year’s Day. It’s a very cold and rainy New Year’s Day here in Southern California, but it’s New Year’s Day, meaning you’re going to be seeing this at the end of January, which means my buffer has shrunk from two months to barely a month. Uh, for lots of reasons: the move, work life. I used to have a two-month buffer. Now I’ve got a one-month buffer, but that’s why I like having a buffer, because the it’s the.

item.title: Finding Meaning In Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about finding meaning in games.

Let’s face it, I’ve made a lot of games. Look at this face. I’ve made a lot of games in the last 45 years. These games have very rich settings, as rich as I can come up with. I remember I always tell you go setting, story, system mechanics. So my games I try to give them as rich a setting as I can, given whatever technical, technological or other limitations that they may have had. And then I give a very long, involved main story but then a lot of NPCs and they all have lots of side stories too. So my games, because of that, my games touch on a lot of different things. There’s a lot of stuff going on and there’s a lot of themes in my games and I even have said there’s one theme that’s in common in all my games. I have a whole video about it, The.

item.title: What Is An Immersive Sim?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about what is an immersive sim.

Definitions are hard. I almost didn’t want to make this video, but Shadow the Cat 7 actually joined my channel as a master member to not only have me answer this, but push it in the queue. So here we go.

Shadow the Cat 7 asks: “I was wondering what is Tim Kane’s definition of an immersive sim? I don’t think I’ve heard you mention them at all, but it seems like there’s a lot of debate around the subject”. There’s not just a lot of debate over what an immersive sim is. There’s a lot of debate about what anything is. I have an entire video devoted to what a cup is.

item.title: Temple Of Elemental Evil Design Highlights
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Temple of Elemental Evil design highlights.

This is a document I found in my archive. I was looking through it for something else, but I thought I’d share this because for all you people out there who are developing games, at some point you’re going to be asked to promote this game, maybe to press or maybe to fans, maybe at a conference or a convention, or maybe in a recorded interview or a playthrough. You should know what is important to talk about at such interviews and events, including what makes your game special or unique or why the press should be interested in it or why a player would want to play it I was asked by Atari to make this list, which they asked me. They said: “Could you make some design highlights”. This was because they were already planning for press events. This is dated May 13th 2003., there was a press event coming up in August, which is just a few months away. I wasn’t going to do that press event. That was going to be somebody else on the team. They had a family emergency. Someone else said they’d do it, but they didn’t have a passport that was valid, so I did it I have a video on this. It’s the Temple of Elemental Evil. Press event Took place in August 2003 in a castle south of London.

item.title: Lessons From Old Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about lessons from old games I’ve never talked about.

This Comes from a good question from you, Hasty 6779 — and I hope I pronounced your name right — Who asks: “Hi Tim, a question that hopefully hasn’t been asked already: What do you think can be learned from old games? Is there any lost wisdom out there”. Yes, there is Good question, Particularly good question because it’s clear and short and to the point. So I really like that question. I’m not going to say I thought I had talked about this, but I do have a lot of other lessons from videos.

item.title: Psychology In Video Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about psychology in video games because a good designer thinks about how people think.

I think about what you think And I know what you’re thinking right now. And yes, I dress myself. I picked this shirt out myself because it’s got popsicles on it.

Some of you may have missed where I talked about the fact that I have a minor in psychology. Weirdly, when I wanted to get computer science at University of Virginia, it’s part of the engineering program And if you pick computer science as your major, they require you to pick a minor and it says related minor. I would say most of the people I knew in computer science took management information systems as their minor, which was in the business school, which made me realize it could be anywhere.

item.title: Timelessness
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about timelessness.

I’m going to start off by talking about how and why I’ve made efforts to make this channel timeless. And then I want to talk about the extent to which I tried to make my games timeless.

It didn’t always succeed. A lot of places, a lot of elements of games, you literally can’t succeed. But you should try, just because you can’t succeed at something doesn’t mean you shouldn’t try. The actual journey is the important part, not the goal.

item.title: Min Player Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about min player spec.

You may wonder what I’m talking about. This is a complicated topic. I’ve put off talking about it literally for years because it is complex and it is nuanced And I’ve thrown out those words before in previous video topics that I thought were had had that nuance to it. People still took the wrong thing, so I’m going to be very clear and careful. Also, this is a topic I don’t talk about with a lot of other game developers because it requires a certain amount of experience actually making games before you get why this is important. I don’t hear game players talk about this much at all.

item.title: Game Punishments
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game punishments, where the player does something in the game and gets punished for it.

This is from a question that someone left in the comments and I’ll read it in a second. But I could have sworn I’d done this. I was absolutely positive. I spent five or 10 minutes searching my channel so I could just link him to the video. Couldn’t find it, and that’s when I realized I think I was insane in the membrane. I’ve never done a video on game punishment.

item.title: Playing Games As Intended
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about playing games as intended.

Sorry, I’ve been watching way too much food wishes with Chef John. I will stop talking like that, I promise I want to talk about playing games as intended. This came from a question I got and I answered it, and then I thought I need to go into more detail. So Abel Anderson asked: “Tim, I have a serious question.

item.title: Tiles, Walking, And Projection
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about tiles, walking and projection And just off the top, let me just say

this is a. I’m talking about 2D because. In 3D, you know, when everything’s polygon, you can do whatever you want. You can choose whatever camera angle you want. You can let players do different angles. I’m talking about 2D games: how you pick how you want your tiles to work, which can be both the tiles that put down the terrain and then the tiles that you walk on, Frequently different, usually different.

item.title: Unneeded Realistic Designs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about unneeded realistic designs.

What I mean by this is I was talking to a friend who’s actually in the game industry — never worked with him. So industry distant colleague — and he asked me a very simple question. He said: “How come you’ve never really had weather in a lot of your games”. And I started saying things like: well, it wasn’t connected, I didn’t connect it to anything And we did other things for visual variation, but really full screen effects can be expensive, whether 2D or 3D, for different reasons, which I’ll talk about. And he said: well, you did time of day in a lot of your games. There was time of day in Fallout, there was time of day in Arcanum. And Arcanum one mattered.

item.title: Traditional Game Mechanics: What I Tried
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about traditional game mechanics and what I tried to do in video games based on those mechanics.

This comes from a question from sherner, who asked: I know You’ made a video game about board games that have influenced you, but I was wondering, since you mentioned reactivity, multiple solution puzzles and roleplay as defining elements of your games. As you said it yourself, all stem back to traditional ttrpg design choices, obviously D and D being our biggest reference. So what other elements of traditional gaming, IE non-digital formats such as board games, have influenced Your Design philosophy and style? Was it a conscious decision to implement them into your games or would you try to distance video games from traditional gaming? Finally, what other elements have you liked to experiment and transposed video games throughout your career, but you couldn’t figure out a way to do it?

item.title: Unpopular Game Opinions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about unpopular game opinions.

What do I mean by that? I’m talking about whether, as a gamer or a game developer, that you like certain things in your games that most people don’t seem to like, or, vice versa, you love certain things in your games that it seems a lot of people don’t like.

Let’s talk about that. I’ve already talked about why you sometimes see games with features that you personally don’t like, and it’s because — and this is hard to accept — it is because you’re in the small group — that there’s a much bigger group of people who really like those features and — trust me, I’ve been there. I tend to like certain features that aren’t the majority that people like, and I’ll talk about that. This may be something that you find hard to see. Maybe you know you don’t like this feature and none of your friends like it either, so why are they putting it in?

item.title: Numerical Item Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about numerical item design.

This comes from a very good question from homicidal pacifist who asked: can you talk a bit about item implementation? How do you decide what types of items go in games, especially those with more unique effects like the shrink ray from The Outer Worlds? For clarification — and I asked for clarification on this — I was thinking about the various ways that items are designed in games, from the more built crafting and grinding for 5% more fire damage approach, like Diablo, to more of your weapon type affects your play style approach, like Monster Hunter. I was wondering if you could maybe share your thoughts about the pros and cons of each for player or developer perspective.

item.title: Alternate Fallout Radiation Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about an alternate Fallout radiation design.

It’s a fun Friday. I mentioned last week in my video on my favorite shipped designs that I had an alternate radiation design for Fallout, but because it never — I never shipped that — it’s not in that list. It also wasn’t rejected.

In fact, this is something I made years later, because, full disclosure, I was never 100% satisfied with how we did radiation in Fallout. It was just damage, um, but later, fallouts — I was also not 100% satisfied. It became a DOT or a Max Health loss or something that was kind of like poison. I didn’t like any of this, so years later — and I’m not 100% sure when, because it’s not dated — it was after I left interplay. It might have even been after fall three came out. I don’t know. It was on loose leaf, undated paper — I came up with a different design that I would have loved to have used, so I thought I’d talk.

item.title: Game Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game time, and by that I mean timed quests, and even more than just timed quests, and also the concept of time in a game.

This comes from a question from fanda 6122, who asked: have you done a video on clocks and in-game time? Yet many modern crpgs don’t force your hand or have any sense of urgency, even the ones that do Overland travel. Well, good question. It raises a lot of — uh, bigger issues than just putting in timed quests, so I kind of wanted to start with that and then get to the bigger issues of time in game. So let’s start with time quests. We did this in Fallout. You know you had to go get the water chip, um, or game over.

item.title: My Favorite Shipped Designs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my favorite shipped designs.

It’s a fun Friday. I want to answer a question from ToastManDennis1218, who asked: could you please make the same format video with your ideas that were accepted, made it into your games and you are happy they did? This was on my video of my ideas that got rejected, which I link below. By the way, this video has a lot of videos in it, and I’ll link them all below. Um, I won’t always be able to give all the context. This might be a long video. I won’t always be able to give all the context for all the design ideas I’ve worked on, but there’s a ton of videos associated with this video that will give you a lot of background on them. So before I dive into my list, though, of things I designed that went into my game that I really liked, I’m going to limit it in two ways, because what would be a Tim?

item.title: "Impossible" Ideas
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about impossible ideas.

I am going to talk about what I mean by impossible at some point in the video and what it probably really means, but I think it’d be more fun for me to talk about examples of that before I say let’s dig into impossible. But this is a question from Fear the clown who, in my video for Unique roles for players video, which I’ll link below, asked: the idea of a boss having its own save game is wild. Can you go into more detail about your thought process for that boss fight?

item.title: Game Length
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game length in particular.

I want to answer a question from bone Bard 6178, who asked: it feels like a lot of people put value on Long games with a lot of content, while I avoid almost anything these days longer than 10 hours. Curious how you feel about the benefits and drawbacks of long and short games. That’s a very good question. I know I I don’t. I know I haven’t talked about this, I know, but various videos of mine I may mention um trying to reach a certain number of hours, because it’s something that used to be more frequently asked for, and I’ll talk about that the way I thought I’d structure this.

item.title: Having Unique Rules For Players
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about having unique rules for players, and

this is me answering a question from shy robot, who asked: in many RPGs it feels like the player is governed by different rules than the rest of the world and its NPCs. What game systems and design processes, do you think really close this division? Let me just say, shy robot, I know what you’re talking about and it is an interesting question because not everybody agrees on even what you mean by the roles and what players can have and NPCs can have, and whether they even agree that players shouldn’t have unique roles. So let’s dive into that first of all. I know what you mean.

item.title: My Ideas That Got Rejected
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my ideas that got rejected from my own games, and I’m going to start all the way back with Fallout, which was now what?

Almost 30 years ago that that project got started and I was Project Lead or design director or game director on a lot of these titles and my own ideas didn’t make it in. So I’ve done a lot of videos where I talk about learning from failure and I talk about how you can learn from other people’s failures. So learn from mine, learn from ideas I wanted to put into games that got thrown out. Let’s start with Fallout.

item.title: Learning Design From Travel
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about learning design from travel.

May seem weird that I’m saying I learned game design lessons from traveling around the world, but I did, and this is one of a series of videos I’ve slid in to the channel. I’ve talked about learning design from failure, from board games, from Christmas gift exchanges, from sitcoms, from collecting fragrances, from other non-game disciplines, even lessons I learned from a bad teacher. Basically, what you should walk away from is you can learn game design from just about anything. So don’t restrict yourself like I did for years, because, let me tell you, I used to be a bad traveler, or, more specifically, I used to think that I hated traveling. Part of this, I think, came from my family, and not deliberately. My family went everywhere by car. We, every Easter, we went to Ohio to spend with my aunt. We took a couple trips to Disney World, which, if you don’t know, Orlando, is about 12 hours by car from Washington DC. I grew up right outside DC. That is a long trip, especially for loading a bunch of kids into a station wagon, and especially if you and some of the other kids get car sick. It was just not pleasant for me to the point where I actually said I didn’t want to go to Disney World the second time because I just didn’t want to go through that, and I basically was. I went against my will.

item.title: WizWar: A Study In Great Board Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about wiiz war, a study in great board game design.

I’m going to talk about this game. This is my copy of wiiz War. It’s the 1991 Edition, there were a lot of different editions out there and I want to talk about this because I think there’s a lot of good lessons you could learn about making a board game from this little teeny board game it’s. I’ve talked about playing this game before. It was in a video about after hours game playing at interplay. I will link it below.

item.title: My Games As Other Genres
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about modeling, constipation in role playing games e.

No, I’m not. Today I’m going to talk about realizing my games into other genres. This is something that I mentioned weeks ago in a video I made that was a postmortem of all my games combined together. It was called meta postmortem.

In the comment section of that video a lot of people mentioned that they wanted me to continue my thought on. I have a lot of ideas in my idea book about the IPS I’ve made, but making games with them that just weren’t role playing games, CU, I got a little, I said, I felt a little pigeon hold into making role playing games, not that I don’t like making them and I don’t have fun making them, but some of my IPS I thought would work better in other genres or just as well, and even though I don’t have any experience making them, I was like one day I wanted to have made them like that but I didn’t. I even got a request from someone in the business saying, hey, keep going with that thought. So this video is me keeping going with that thought.

item.title: Game Design Portfolio
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about your game design portfolio or, more specifically, what you, as a game designer, should include in a portfolio that you’re sending to a game company when you apply for a job.

This is me answering gamedev Tommy, who asked for — quote advice on what a game design portfolio should really consist of and how it should present, be presented. For someone who has not worked in a mainstream Studio yet, unquote — good question, um, so let me talk about exactly what I want to see. I’m going to divide it into what I expect to see and what I’d really like to see. So the expect — the expectation — part is: you really should include this. The I like part is: if you got this, it’s what I want to see, and it’ll move you to the top of the pile. Before you start, though, you should probably.

item.title: Fast Travel
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fast travel.

Now this is a topic I I thought I had covered this, but when I went and looked through my videos, I basically briefly mentioned it in a video about Quest markers, which I’ll link below, but I didn’t actually talk about it that much and not into the detail that I think some people wanted. In fact, I got a question from a snow crash who asked: have you spoken about fast travel on RPGs and some games? I feel like you can use it to essentially bypass things like encumbrance, hardcore mechanics, Random Encounters, Etc. Yes, you are absolutely right, you can use it. Many games let you, especially after you buy a couple perks, avoid a lot of the mechanics that make the game more difficult. So let’s talk about that.

item.title: Game Simplification
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game simplification, and by this I’m talking about various ways games can be made easier to use but to some players feel handh holdy.

This is me answering a question from VA cus who asks: recently I saw some screenshots from games with very obvious Clues where to go next. I mean like obvious gray rock formations with obvious yellow protrusions to stick to ancient ladders painted in yellow, Etc. I don’t actually know from what games those were.

item.title: Intent
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about intent, and I will get to how this refers to G video games, but I wanted to talk about it because it’s come up a lot in a lot of my videos.

In fact, it’s often the subtext of many of my videos and I will often say upfront: you know, this is what I’m going to talk about and this is why — and I tell stories about why I’m telling these things, or I talk about why I’m saying something before I even go into what I’m saying and why I thought this was going to be a serious video. Of course, right when I’m setting everything up, the dog decides this is a perfect time to fall asleep at my feet. So you’re going to have to listen to my seriousness with the background of Darth Vader dog.

item.title: Design Fallbacks
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about design fallbacks now.

I’ve talked about these before and in fact I’m answering a question from gkid 100 who asked: can you make a video about fallbacks? What is the process for designing a less resource heavy version of a system or a replacement for a system? I think I talked about this. I know I did design fallbacks heavily during The Outer Worlds, but I had them in previous games, just not so structured like. There literally was a section in the design document for the The Outer Worlds. It’s called design fallbacks and, by the way, when I talk about design document there are some people out there that still think I’m talking about I have a paper document or a word dock. The Outer Worlds one was spread across multiple, uh, web pages. I, we used Confluence that were constantly Ed, Ed and interl, so it was like a living document. So you know, if you went to a page you could not only see the design. But you could see people’s comments on it. You could see who was assigned to it. If you clicked on that, you could go through even the schedules and see idea of when this thing would be made.

item.title: Balancing Friction
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about balancing friction, and this comes from a question from padon Roso — I hope I’m pronouncing your name correctly — who asked: can you talk about balancing friction in RPGs and games?

It seems like quite the complex thing to do, when the friction points for the player and how they’re distributed around the gameplay Loop will most likely influence other already complex systems in the game. For example, I’m thinking about pacing, both the storytelling and the alternation of action, non-action faces. I’m also talking about game economy, as talked about in the encumbrance video, and the full effect of friction points seems to me like something you can only fully evaluate when the game is all playable, especially when the crossover with those other systems I was referring to earlier is taken into account. Oh wait, there’s more. Do you have any trick or story you can tell about this, about how you tackle this problem along your career?

item.title: My Games’ Theme
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my games theme.

That’s games plural and theme singular, because I want to talk to you about how you can look at all the games I’ve made, especially the ones I was fundamentally involved with creating the original Ip, but also ones that I worked on, and how you can interpret them through the single lens of one theme. Now you may be thinking my, theme is choice and consequence, but I view that less as a theme and more as a mechanic. So I’ve talked about setting, story mechanics.

item.title: Encumbrance, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about encumbrance again.

So this is encumbrance part two. The video — uh, the part one video, just called incumbrance, and I’ll link it below — generated a lot of really good discussion in the comment section. So if you haven’t watched it, you probably want to go watch that before you watch this, because I’m going to build on some things from the first part and I’m not really going to dive into details. So if you haven’t watched encumbrance, just stop this, go watch that and then come back here. The first thing I noticed was a lot of people were saying how I was talking about encumbrance as weight based. So items had weight, you could only carry a maximum weight and, um, there were things like perks and attributes that could increase that weight and they suggested doing it with slots and there’s multiple ways of doing it I mean, our Arcanum had a grid and you had to fit everything on that grid and sometimes you had to Tetris things around to get a new object, and there are other games that did you have like everything. Every item just fits into one slot, and you start with 20 slots, and maybe there are bags you can find that increase your slots. Maybe there are perks that say every bag you pick up has two extra slots, whatever it is the My point stands, though, that encumbrance is about imposing an inventory limit about how much the player can carry. So whether you do it with weight or whether you do it with slot, whether it’s multiple slots, whether it’s slot and weight, those are just limits. So we’re still talking about the same thing. It’s just: how is your encumbrance set up to limit the number of items that a player could carry at any given point? Then several of you said, well, just don’t have.

item.title: Learning Game Design From Old Sitcoms
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about learning game design from old sitcoms.

Now I’m going to tell you why I’m talking about this and then how it connects to video games — kind of at the end. But the last few years have been interesting. I probably watched more television in the last three years than I have in the last 30, and for a whole bunch of reasons. One, I was working part-time. Two to: I had moved to a new city where I didn’t know anybody, in a new state where I didn’t know anybody, and I did it that all in the middle of a pandemic. I moved up here in the middle of 2020, so I just had a lot of free time on my hands and nowhere I could go. So I ended up watching a lot of TV shows.

item.title: Balancing Game Features
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about balancing game features.

Now this is a question from Josh and Parker who asked: could you go over the process of balancing new game features as they’re added to the game? How do you make sure new weapons or spells aren’t overpowered but the player is still able to get enough new content to stay entertained? How do you know how many hits an enemy should take to kill Etc?

item.title: Horror In Non-Horror Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about horror and non horror games.

I want to answer a question from decode 666, who asked horror and non-horror video games — how to approach it properly, that one Mission which still ST, which still sends chills down your spine, for example, the Ocean House Hotel in vampire, the Masquerade bloodlines, or the atmosphere of Fallout 1 and two, like the at Ambience, music level, design and art, robbing the cradle in Thief, deadly Shadows, the Dunwich building in Fallout 3, ravenol and halflife 2. Those are a lot of good examples and I think I’ve played them all or been there when they were made. So I’m going to answer decode 666’s question, but first I want to point out decode 66 is the person who did the, wood art behind me, the one I did the video on. I’ll link it below, so so, hey, anyway, um, a lot of what you mentioned in your question, even if it was on games I worked on, I didn’t. Make it, so I’ve told this story many times, but Jason Anderson did the Ocean House level in Bloodlines.

item.title: Encumbrance
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about encumbrance.

I feel like I’ve talked about encumbrance a lot, but it turns out I talked about it here and there in a bunch of different videos, but I don’t actually have a video about incumbrance. And I want to answer a question from clown Hustler 5815 who asks: would you share your thoughts on carry weight mechanics? I’m thinking something on the conflict between considerations like limiting the players’s progression, economy, role playing versus realism, versus forcing the player to basically stop their game and play inventory Tetris, run around to Merchants meticulously, compare weight value ratios or go throw the item on a loot Goblin pile. That’s a long question. I do have answers.

item.title: Code The Rules, Script The Exceptions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about coding rules for your game and using scripts for the exceptions.

Now, this is something that I’ve talked about. A lot came up most recently in a video I did on using Game Mechanics for storytelling, and in the comments for that video, infinite Warrior, who I think is like all the other Warriors but uncountably bigger, asked me to explain — quote - how utilizing the mechanical as opposed to the scripted approach might be able to mitigate some of that seemingly necessary kind of wonkiness — unquote - basically talking about bizarre States you can get into in some games. Now I’ve linked that video Below on Mechanics for storytelling, but I’ve also linked my video on emergent game play and another one on how to do mul, how to get multiple Quest Solutions out of your game more easily, and they all basically rely on the approach I mentioned in the title, which is try to get your system mechanics coded as simply and as directly as possible. Try to cover almost all the cases so that the rare exception is what gets scripted, not tons of the game being scripted. Script should be used surgically, saying, oh, in this one case we’re going to do this thing.

item.title: Content As Reward
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about content as reward.

Now this is a really great topic. It was suggested by ulyses saeo, who said a suggestion for a video content as reward. This is a major overlooked thing in Game Dev, in my humble opinion, which I feel massively. Railroad player action: I agree. Uh, it’s.

I can’t believe I’ve never talked about this and you’re right, I’ve never. I never have, so let me talk about what it is, why I like it, why you don’t see it much, and a possible solution which you may not like. So let me Define what I think ulyses meant by this and what I’m going to take it as meaning.

item.title: Beyond Fighting, Sneaking, and Talking
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to brainstorm ideas with you on ways RPGs can move past the fighting, sneaking, talking Triad that, let’s face it, has been done to death since the 90s.

I mean, yeah, that’s kind of what we pushed really hard with. Fallout was: can you fight past things, can you sneak past things, can you talk past things? But Fallout was made in 1997.

item.title: Gameplay Options
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gameplay options now.

A few months ago I did a video on game difficulty settings and talked about the pros and cons of different difficulty settings you should put in the game. But in the comment section a bigger discussion happened about just gameplay options in general — way more than just difficulty settings — and one of the things that came up was people talking about games that have a lot of gameplay settings. Gameplay options now there are people who like them and they like them for the obvious reasons of you know. I like to be able to customize the game exactly how, I want to play it, which I get, and I probably fall in that category a lot more than the people who do not like having lots of gameplay options.

item.title: Game Flavor
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game flavor, and I’m deliberately doing this on a Friday, Funday, because what I’m going to talk about is very subjective.

So kind of view: this, as a Tim talks about game development at another, very high level of description. Uh, and I almost didn’t do this video because what I mean by game flavor is really hard to describe. I’m going to try to do it, but it’s hard.

item.title: Core Game Loops
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about core game Loops.

This is something that’s absolutely fundamental to game development. I’m incredibly embarrassed that I’ve had this channel for more than a year and I’ve never talked about this. And I bring it UPC. I bring that up because it’s so fundamental. I don’t even think about it.

I’ve mentioned this a few times about other things that it doesn’t occur to me to talk about certain things because it’s just so like endemic to the process and I’ve been doing it for so long. I don’t even doesn’t even occur to me that this is something I should mention. But I was looking at something online and they were talking about core game loops and I’m like, oh my goodness, I have not even mentioned core game Loops. So let’s talk about this, because this is a core game Loop.

item.title: High Level Design Direction
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about highlevel design Direction.

This is something that the game director, creative director, whatever it’s called — on your project. This is probably the single most important task that the person in this role does. I’m going to talk about it, but it’s also something that I think is misunderstood or underestimated by a lot of people who aren’t in the role and by people who first step into the role. Um, and what I mean by highle Design direction is you basically have to watch everything going into the game, everything: system mechanics, story elements, quests, Maps, dialogue, NPC, the names of things, lore, text, everything — to make sure it all fits. And that highle — whatever view, whatever you know your take on — your vision of this game should be: all that stuff has to fit and serve that Vision.

item.title: Selling A Game Design Spec
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about selling a game design spec.

This is actually from a question I got from Nash kruka, I hope, kka. I’m hope I’m pronouncing that right. Um, I’ll say Nash from now on, because it looks like you may have changed your name on the account since when you asked this question and when I’m answering it. But Nash asked: if someone had the entire Wiki for a game like Fallout New Vegas and approached a studio, would it even mean anything to them? Ideas are cheap, but what is a fully thought out game iname Universe worth?

item.title: Unused GDC 1999 Roadtrip Presentation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about RPG 2000.

This is a presentation that I was going to give at the 1999 GDC road trip in Austin. Let me give you some context. I was going through my archives and on the inside — the archive of the very first machine I had at troyka — I think I had three different machines — I found a PowerPoint called GDC 99 road trip. I remember making this PowerPoint vaguely, but I did not go to the GDC 99 road trip convention in Austin, which leads me to believe — because, by the way, there were no other notes, no notes on this talk, no notes about the talk, no emails back and forth — that either I made this and never submitted it, or I submitted it or didn’t get in, or maybe even I got in and decided not to go. I have no memory, and what’s weird about this is I would have been making this in early summer of 99, as if I wasn’t busy enough. We were.

item.title: Fallout As A Work Of Art
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout as a work of art, and just before I get started, I’m talking about the whole series.

I’m talking about all the games and the TV show. I’m talking about it as an IP. Um, I’ve already done a video about games as art. I’ll link it below. You can kind of see this video as a continuation of that video, because I’m taking some of my ideas from that video and I’m kind of talking about it in more detail. Um, also, I want to clarify that making a game, actual game development — not the end product, but the development that itself — is both an art and a science, and I mean I this is something that is behind all the videos I do, and I’m constantly talking about this, so I’m not going to rehash it, but I will say this: people who try to treat Tre, the development process, like it was Art, are shocked when money factors in, are like: oh my.

item.title: Deep Learning
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about deep learning.

Now I’m going to tell you what I mean by that and where I learned about that. But as a quick aside, I did a video once about how I do humor in games and I said I often embed humor in my games in such a way that the people get it, get it and feel included. But the people who don’t get it don’t even know there was a joke and they don’t feel excluded. So I’m going to rip away the wall for a second here, the way I said deep learning. There are a few people that I used to hang out with at interplay that right now are going, but most of you are like I have no idea.

item.title: Learning Design From Other Disciplines
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about learning design from other disciplines, and by disciplines I can mean Hobbies, activities, anything you do besides video games.

So this is an answer to the question from Ezra Stanton, 8685, who asks: I’m curious, what other artistic disciplines do you think have the most to teach us about game design and what did it teach you about game design? Examples: drawing, painting, writing, animation, dancing, making, music, cooking, Etc. Ooh, that’s a good question. Um, I’ll talk about some of my hobbies or other things I do, and then I’ll talk to about, kind of how that all comes together in my design aesthetic, my design motifs — I’m not sure what I’d call it. Um, first of all, let’s talk about reading. I do a lot of reading, um, I’ve talked about some of it already on here, and books are amazing in that, you know, they can go anywhere, describe anything. In that sense they’re beyond movies, they’re beyond TV shows and because of that I get. A lot of neat ideas, especially when I’m reading some novel story with an interesting setting or interesting mechanic and I think, how would I do that in a video game? I talk about this in the video I have about Roger El’s Lord of Light and how I would turn it into a crpg.

item.title: Games As Actions
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about games as actions, and I’ll talk about what I mean by that.

But first I want to talk about the film director, Deni fenov. I really like his movies — you know arrival Dune. I like his cinematography, I like his approach to making movies, I like that his movies — and he’s talked about this — are about exploring the human condition, about making us think about big issues, and also that his movies are open — open to interpretation. I mean that’s like a checkbox of everything I like. But he had a quote that I heard recently and I really liked it, but it made me think a lot about games, and here’s the quote: I strive for visual storytelling, for images that convey meaning without relying on dialogue. Now, what I love about that is not only it a great line, but it also points out a way that movies transcend books. I mean books are great. I read a ton of books, I have a lot of books. I love reading them, when I read them I, you know, imagine all kinds of things in my head. But books are primarily words and when you are talking about books you tend to quote them. You tend to quote lines that you love, even if the lines are making you imagine something or lines are transporting you somewhere. Books are basically remembered from for having great lines.

item.title: Understanding Game Design Choices
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about understanding game design choices.

Specifically, I want to talk about why designers sometimes choose the feature set that they have for their game, and I want to talk about it because there are a lot of comments I see on the channel from people basically saying I don’t like this, I wish they hadn’t done this, was a bad choice, and it’s kind of like in that video I had about bad games. I want to talk about why games are sometimes made with choices the way they are that you yourself do not like and do not agree with. Um, now, remember I’ve said frequently too many videos to link to that. Whenever you have a question about something, the answer is money. So you know, why is this game like this money? Why didn’t they do this money? Why did they do this money? So, in essence, my answer for this question of why this design Choice was made is probably money, um.

item.title: Influential Books
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about influential books.

Now I’ve already talked about tabletop influences on my games during my career and what games I think are really good for you to learn from, along with my top five favorite games, and I separated those into different videos because I thought that was important to do, even though some people still think the ones that I think are good to learn from are my favorites. Um, but today I wanted to talk about what books exist that really influenced me, and some of them I read a long time ago. Some of them I’ve read recently. All of them I’ve reread recently. These are books that have stuck with me since when I read them originally.

item.title: Bartle's Player Archetypes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about B’s player archetypes, which is also known as the ble taxonomy of players.

This is in response to someone, J extra 1313, who asks: hi, Tim, there’s a well-known rule that there’s four types of players: Killers, social players, explorers and Achievers — the ble taxonomy of player types. Therefore, every game should have things in it: appease each player type to bring in the largest audience possible. How true is this taxonomy in your experience, and is following this philosophy helpful or harmful? Well, I’m glad you asked because this is something that I’ve known about for decades and I kind of agree with and I will talk about that. And, by the way, there’s a whole bunch of really interesting psychology. Um, you can read to learn more about video games. In fact, there is a book I read in the 80s by George and Elizabeth Loftus called the psychology of video games and even though, yeah, it was talking about Pac-Man and Space Invaders — all of it still applies today, so highly recommend that book.

item.title: Why I Hate Cultural References
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I hate cultural references.

Now, I almost said why I hate them in games, but I also hate them in TV shows and movies, pretty much any of my entertainment books. So let me talk about what I mean by the references and why I don’t like them, even just not in games, and what my advice would be for you to do if you absolutely cannot bear not having a comment about something. So when I mean cultural references, I just don’t mean you making reference to a song you like or a movie you like or a book. I’m talking about anything. Anytime you reference anything cultural, a famous person, uh, a band, a movie, a book, a TV show, an event.

item.title: Managing Game Expectations
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about managing game expectations, and let me explain what I mean by that.

I’ve already done a video on discoverability, which is the about the difficulty of people finding, about finding your game even exists, and all the videos I mentioned. I’ll put links in below. This is kind of the opposite of that I’m talking about.

Once someone finds your game, how do you let them know what kind of game it is they’re getting? And, just so you know, this isn’t the expectations that your team would have you. So I’ve already talked about maintaining a good vision for your game in my videos on how to be a good game director and how to write good design pillars.

item.title: Games Without End & New Game Plus
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game endings and new game plus — two different things.

They’re kind of related. I’ll explain why I have two different questions I’m answering here. I’ll talk about the first one, talk for a bit and then, when I talk about new game plus, I’ll bring in the other person who asked. The first one is from Ian akamoto, who writes: would love to hear your thoughts on games that end and return the player to the menu upon completing the main quest line, versus games that allow the player to continue playing on. Wondering if you favor a specific camp and how a developer might approach this Choice. Okay, I think it’s pretty obvious that I favor the Game ending Choice, since it’s pretty much what my games all do. Um, part of the reason I did this is when you have an ending.

item.title: What Is Fun?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about fun.

I’ve already done a video, and I’ll link it below, about balancing fun and realism in games, but I’ve got a lot of fun oriented questions. People want to know what I think about fun versus balance, what I think about games that force you to go, what the designer considers to be the F path, or just what is fun, Define fun. I’m going to take a stab at it same way I took a stab at def: finding what an RPG is. Let’s see how this goes, first of all, let’s talk about fun versus balance. I don’t think anybody’s going to be surprised that I prefer fun overbalance in any of my games, and you can even see some of my games were really unbalanced.

item.title: Designing Mechanics First
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about the idea of Designing mechanics first for your game before doing anything else.

This is actually in response to a question from this is fine. Who asks: you’ve mentioned time and again that it goes setting, then story and finally system mechanics. But what about the approach that some directors use, like Miyazaki Ora, to design mechanics, movement levels, ET first and then develop a fitting story based on one, the lore established in the background and two, the things that organically emerge while developing the game itself?

item.title: Easter Eggs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Easter eggs specifically.

I want to talk about Easter eggs that are in games that I worked on, that have something to do with me or that I’ve talked about before, but I don’t see very widespread that people have either talked about the Easter egg but not really much about it, which, by the way, is interesting. I find a lot of um people online love to talk about games as if the people involved with them are gone, are just they’re no longer around, and then they guess at things and they’re like we don’t know why this happened or it’s. I literally watched a video the other day that was like 10 big mysteries about early games, and by early games he met games in the mid to late 90s and he was wrong on most of them. I was like that isn’t why that was done that way, and they weren’t even games I had worked on. It was just I knew about them, so it was very strange. Um, and I get that a lot of them were probably clickbait, but these are Easter eggs that I kind of want to give more information about, just because they meant something to me. First one want to talk about is an Easter egg.

item.title: Explaining Non-Linearity
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about explaining nonlinearity and how this used to be really hard.

It’s a lot easier now still has some challenges. This is an answer to a question I got on the channel by Mr Lars cot1, who asks: you mentioned Force linearity as mistake number five in your 2016 reboot talk. Can you elaborate on how you’ve been challenged on The Stance during your career? It’s interesting how one of your main RPG design pillars might conflict with the views of other team members — Naro designers, cinematic creators, other programmers, Etc. And do you find that developing open-ended sandbox RPGs have become much more accepting by people in the game industry over the years? So, first off, that’s a good question and I thought I should begin by showing a clip of this talk just to kind of get everyone the same page about what Lars is referring to. So here it is, so the next uh problem I’ve had: um, I have a thing for linearity.

item.title: Game Difficulty Settings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game difficulty.

Specifically, I got a question from Noah Calder 4279, who asks: I’d love if you could share your thoughts on difficulty in RPGs. Do you think customizable difficulties such as settings and Sliders work well in RPGs? Which are the best methods of making RPGs difficult, and should RPGs get easier or more difficult the further a player advances through the game? That is a set of good questions that all fall under game difficulty. So I thought, okay, time to do a game difficulty video. So just up front, I do like customizable difficulty settings. I think anything that makes a player turn the game into something they’d like more I’m not against. Frequently in my career I’ve had arguments with people — designers, programmers — who think that every time you put an option in a game it’s because you couldn’t make a decision, and I see where they’re coming from. There were sometimes I couldn’t decide what was better and I thought, maybe.

item.title: Achievements
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about achievements.

I’ve gotten a lot of questions over the last several months about achievements, mainly asking me whether I like them or whether I don’t like them, what I think of them, and I’ve thought about this a lot because I was trying to figure out how to say it, how to crystallize my thoughts, because I know, like with a lot of my other videos, people are going to pick and choose what they hear in it and you know, waiting for, you know, an article. Tim Kane hates achievements, but let me tell you this: is my high level: I have a love hate relationship with achievements and to understand why you kind of have to look back probably 30 years for me to understand, like, how I view achievements. So in the ’90s and even the early 2000s, I was doing RPGs that had a lot of tracker variables. That’s how I did endgame slides. That’s how I did story reactivity. That’s how I had NPCs just react to the player — um, even if it’s just a bark over their head going — you don’t have clothes on — kept track of lots of variables. Sometimes they’d look at the player and look at values on them, but sometimes variables would be tracked globally. They did this thing, the player said this thing, the player found this thing.

item.title: "Bad Games"
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about bad games — and I’m laughing even saying that, because I think it’s such a weird term and it can mean so many different things.

And just before I even dive into it, bear in mind I’ve already done a video on what I think makes a game good, which I’ll link below, and the data, the objective data — on my games, which I’ll also link below. So I kind of referen both of those in here, but you might want to watch them first to see where I’m going from in this one.

item.title: Emergent Gameplay
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about emergent gameplay.

Jeffrey Sherman, 609, asks. Recently I have become entranced with emergent gameplay. After a bit of thinking on it, I feel like a fair amount of your games have offered it and I’m curious if it is considered when making a game and if it is, how much? And is it style dependent?

Uh, crpg, arpg, Etc. I like this question. I’m actually surprised I haven’t really talked about it. Um, I certainly haven’t made it the topic of a video, but I mean, I just start off by saying I love emergent gameplay and that’s obviously why it exists in all of my games. It’s something I saw happen on tabletop and it happens just so organically there. That was something I wanted to make happen in my computer games.

item.title: The Four Steps For Fixing Design Problems
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about fixing design problems.

These are not bugs. I’m not talking about the game is, you know, near in completion and there are things that aren’t working right. I’m talking about you make a design, give it to your programmers and they’re like we gotcha, and they implement it and the artists all go: here’s your good stuff, put it in, here’s everything you asked for and it’s all in and working exactly the way you asked. But there’s a problem, now I’m going to use an example from the original Fallout game — uh, design problem that it shipped with, so I can use that as an example as we’re going through. So in the original Fallout you could buy an energy weapon skill. In fact, you could tag it right at the beginning of the game if you wanted to. The only problem is energy weapons aren’t available for at least the first half and closer to the first 2/3 of the game.

item.title: Game Design Specification Sections
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about all the different design specifications that a single game may need, because about a month ago I gave an example of how to write a design specification and gypsum generation asks: what are all the design specifications that a game might need?

Okay, that is a very long answer, so grab your coffee. Also, I’m going to put a big caveat on this and say the answer really depends on the game you’re making, because different games will have different sections in their design specification. So let’s pretend we’re making a fantasy RPG and I will tell you what the design specs should include. So I’ll break it down into. I’ll go down to what all the main sections of the design specification are and then for each one of those sections I’ll talk about subsections they should have. Why don’t you take a look at my writing design specifications for exactly the kind of details you will have to put in each one of those subsections?

item.title: Taking Games Seriously
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about taking games seriously.

I actually want to answer a question from rvic, although I’m wondering if that’s rvic asks: can games be taken too seriously? And my short answer is: yeah, sure, anything can be taken too seriously, but I want to make a longer answer than that. I almost just wrote yes and as a comment, but I want to take it, make a longer answer because, first of all, you’re asking me there’s very little I take seriously. Um, usually, on anything I’m willing to concede and go okay, um, but to make my answer clearer, I love when people deep dive into the lore and the setting and mechanics of my games. In fact, when we’re making it, that’s often we’re thinking about people doing that. We want people to do that, um it’s.

item.title: Thoughts On A Temple Remake
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my thoughts on a temple remake, a remake of Temple of Elemental Evil, with things I would like to do that either I ran out of time to do or wish

I had done differently. Um, I’ve already talked about my thoughts on a Fallout remake — that was months ago and an Arcanum remake yesterday, so I thought I’d do a temple remake. I’ll link those other remakes below. I’m not going to do a remake video for vampire Bloodlines, though it’s not really mine to remake thoughts from the about. A remake on that should come from Leonard bski or Jason Anderson. So I’m just GNA complete my Trio of remake Thoughts with temple today. So let’s get started. I the first thing I’d do just the standard.

item.title: Thoughts On An Arcanum Remake
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about thoughts on an Arcanum remake.

Uh, I’ve had different questions about it I even touched upon this a little bit in the video I did on Arcanum as a Windows 10 app, where I actually have Arcanum up and running in Windows 10, and I talked about some things I might do about it, but I didn’t finish the thought. I didn’t do it as completely as I did say in the video called thoughts on a Fallout remake. So I’ll link those two videos below, but I want to do this one, which it will kind of collect all my thoughts on.

item.title: Story Vs Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Story versus game design or, as sherner asks, what is more important for a game, story or game design?

I know, ideally there should be a balance between the two, but that is so rare to find. What’s your view on it? Sorry, I got a tickle in my throat today. So let me say first of all, this is a good question, but maybe not for the reason you think I’m going to say it’s a good question.

item.title: Analyzing Game Designs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about analyzing games.

Now I’ve talked about this before. I’ve mentioned on this channel that when I play video games these days I can’t turn off the analysis part of my brain. So frequently I find myself enjoying games less than I should just because I’m picking apart things and I’ve said I don’t want to go into specifics about Gam I do this with. But bengala fraka asks: could you make a video on how to analyze games critically with the objective of learning more about design?

item.title: Writing Design Specifications
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about how to write design specifications.

Specifically, I’m going to answer a question from Thor RoR TV who asks: I was trying to write a design document for a solo project I’m working on and was wondering if you had any examples of how to properly write a specification that might be given to a programmer or an artist. Also, are there any pitfalls to avoid when writing speci ifications? That is a very good question, thoro TV, and it’s also a little hard to answer because there’s a wild, wildly huge range of design specifications. You might be writing a might be writing, you might be writing a system mechanic. You might be writing a user interface, you might be writing a — um, some narrative guidelines.

item.title: Beginnings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the beginning of a game.

I want to talk about why that’s so important. I’m going to answer a question from Shadow Dragon Dev, who asks: what are your thoughts on what makes a good or bad beginning for a game? Beginning not just as when you press new game, but also as in you first open the game program itself, that entire first impression a player gets at the very start of their experience with it.

item.title: My Work Pattern For Designing
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my work pattern for Designing, and you can consider this a companion video to the one I did on programming, which I’ll put a link in the description.

This is actually answering a question from post nuke Productions, who asks: you’ve done a few videos about what it’s like to program a game, so could you give us an idea of what a day working as a designer is like? I like this question, um, I’ll clarify it, though that I’m going to write down what I assume it’s like. Being a system designer. A level designer and a narrative designer would have a very different day. Level designers would work with artists more. Uh, narrative designers would be more interested in the quests and the storyline and spend a lot of their Day writing characters. So I’m going to go as if it’s a system mechanic designer, um, because that’s what I’m most familiar with. By the way, you might want to check out the work pattern. For programming, because a lot of things I’m going to mention here are very similar to what I do when I’m a programmer. It’s not that I view the same jobs as similar, it’s just when I’m, it’s just me how I do a job.

item.title: Insignificant Features (That I Really Like)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about features that I really notice in games.

In fact, let me I want to answer a question from Mike Z 6289, who asks: what is a feature you see in games that excites you but would seem insignificant to the average gamer? I think that’s a really good question, although I’m not sure exactly who the average gamer is. But before I answer, let me just talk about, like, how I’m going to interpret this question. Um, I interpret this as, like, what features are personal to me that I notice and I really like, but are insignificant, and I think that’s an important term. Um, for example, colorblindness support is not insignificant to me. I need it. Um, to a lot of people they don’t know or care about that feature, Fe, but I can tell you there have been games that came out recently that are big games that supposedly were quite enjoyable, that I literally could not play because they involved solving puzzles with color.

item.title: Ambiguous Grief
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about ambiguous grief.

You may be wondering — well, you may be wondering — why it’s so dark. It’s because it’s not even 7: am. Uncle Tim has to go downtown today, so I have to film This Before Sunrise.

But you’re probably wondering why I’m talking about ambiguous grief, because it’s something that has to do with games. I finally found the psychological term that explains why I dislike certain features in role playing games, but I will get there first. I better explain what I’m talking about, so you probably know what regular grief is. When you lose, grief is. Regular grief is when you lose something that you had. So grief is what you feel when a loved one passes away, or you have a breakup, or you realize you’re losing touch with a friend of yours and you guys just aren’t as good friends anymore, or anything that involves that you had something and now you realize you don’t have it anymore. That’s grief, but ambiguous grief, which is sometimes, I think, called ambiguous loss, but I like the term ambiguous grief better. Ambiguous grief happens when you come to the realization that you will never have something that you always wanted or, Worse, something that you expected to have, so maybe for you it’s hard to say what it would be. For you, maybe you thought you’d own a house by the time you were 35, or maybe you thought you’d get a college degree, or maybe you thought you’d get a game industry job, and you’re coming to the realization that that isn’t going to happen for you.

item.title: Why I Play Games I Don't Like
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about why I play games I don’t like.

Now I don’t review games, and I’ve made a whole video about it with all the reasons why I didn’t review it. Please watch it if you haven’t. Um, I get lots of request to review games or just stream myself playing it, which is going to lead to me making comments, and I don’t do that. What I do want to talk about in this video, though, is, first of all, I play a lot of games. I play games every day — if not no, I’m sure. I play game — video games — every day, I think. I also play, at least every week, a card or board or puzzle game, and sometimes, included in those are ones I simply do not like. Sometimes, I have many, many hours in those games that I do not like. In fact, I had to privatize my Steam account a few years ago because there were different people, colleagues — uh people — in the industry, um fans, um people on Game Dev boards — who would look at the hours I was putting into different games and make all kinds of assumptions about what those hours meant. And let me just tell you that many of those assumptions, many of the things they said, were absolutely wrong.

item.title: Game Pacing
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game pacing.

DG 135 says I think it would be a really interesting to know about your perspective and experience on the difficulty of pacing open World Games, both in its narrative and gameplay. I thought that was a really neat comment. It’s not even a question, uh, just comment that I’m now G to comment on um, open world games are difficult to do — game pacing. Let me talk about game pacing first. I view game pacing as a set of highs and lows that happen while the player goes through the game. Mostly I see this as Combat versus non-combat combat, or what people call downtime. Downtime could be anything from exploration to crafting, to going back to town for using vendors, or just going back to your home or home base or whatever you have in the game to drop stuff off and change companions. Do whatever you do at a home base. In general, I think that’s right, although there are some exciting things that can happen outside side of combat, and also combat itself can be paced.

item.title: Fun vs. Realism
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game design: fun versus realism, and this has come up in the past.

I’ve often said in other videos: I like, I like making games to be fun. I don’t care about realism, I don’t care about balance. I mean I do, but to me, fun is far more important and that’s what I wanted to talk about. I want to talk about what I mean specifically in different parts of a game.

item.title: Board Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about board games — games Charlie K 5964 asks: do you ever play board games?

If so, what do you look for in board games you enjoy versus video games or tabletop games? That’s good question. I really haven’t talked about them much, but I know people have seen them on the bookcases. So I’ll start with saying I play board games a lot more when I was younger, especially when I was like a kid before I got into d my brother — he’s two years older than me — he and I used to play board games all the time when we were kids. We used to play Risk and life and Monopoly, just you know all the classics — and I was so into it that when I was 10 I actually even made a board game.

item.title: Game Economy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game economy.

This is partially based on the video I did on equipment durability, but specifically because a YouTube — YouTube, YouTube — commenter, anen filipovic- 2851 — asks money, imbalance is a huge deal. Having lots of money like screw McDuck after 30% of gameplay makes game super easy, even on the hardest difficulty. So I do not get how this is not super important to fix before shipment. So that made me want to talk about game economy and how hard it is. I could lecture. This could be a whole College course. I mean, you’ve taken economy. Game economy is equally difficult.

item.title: Procedural vs. Handmade Content
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about procedural versus handmade content in games.

Of course I’ve got some preface to all that. First of all, I’m going to call it handmade. I do not like the word bespoke. Bespoke implies handmade and unique, where, when I think about handmade content. True, some of handmade content is only used once in a game, like a particular dialogue with an NPC, but some handmade content is used on a variety of NPCs, such as barks.

item.title: Thoughts On A Fallout Remake
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question from Evan shantlin Who, full disclosure, worked on QA on Fallout.

And Evan asks: hey, Tim, ever thought about what changes or updates you’d like to see in a Fallout remake? Would you want a fully explorable map or Base building added to the game? Are there errors you’d like to see completely reworked or just flushed out?

item.title: Durability Mechanics
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer another question about weapon durability — ice dog 111 asks: I was wondering if you could do a video about weapon durability or equipment durability as a game mechanic.

Is it an outdated mechanic or is it still valid? PS who came up with the weapon to storing golems in Arcanum, the Black Mountain Clan mines? Gollum Gauntlet still gives me nightmares. Okay, okay, I’m going to answer that last question first, but I’m gonna put a big caveat on it I don’t have any note that specifically says who did it, but my brain hole tells me with 90 certainty it was Chad Moore. I believe he did most of the Black Mountain plan Minds, so I think he put that in there. It’s not a system mechanic in the game. The breakage — it was put as a script on the golems and our source control — we used. The version that I have that I preserved in my archive — doesn’t say who did it doesn’t. Have the first person to check it in or the last person to check it in. All I have is my name, because I ended up making a copy of the latest version of that archive, the shipped version. So I don’t know for sure, but I think it was Chad Moore. Anyway, let’s talk about weapon or equipment durability in general. I don’t think it’s outdated.

item.title: Creeping Normality
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about creeping normality.

It’s not the name of my favorite band. It’s not the name of a new game company I’m starting. It is a term that means getting major change made to Something in small, tiny increments. This is not the same as slippery slope, which is a argumentative fallacy where people assume that if you let one little thing in, it will lead to Major unintended consequences. Creeping normality is a deliberate thing that people do where some limits have been defined on, like a Min and a Max of something, and then you push, or other people push on the limits of those, usually the upper limit, trying to get something just past it, and if they do, that moves the limit and then they do it again and it moves the limit and it does again.
